blocks.text: Dear Ms. Little:

Thank you for the walk along the frontage last month and for the older survey material you sent afterward. This letter confirms the terms on which Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC will carry out a sediment sampling program for Salazar-Mendoza. It states the scope, the approach, the people who will do the work, the fee, and the standard terms we work under. If it also states your understanding, please sign where indicated and return one copy to me. A countersigned letter is what we treat as authorization. We do not open a job number without one, and we would rather settle the wording now than argue about it after a sample is in a jar.
Scope of Work

The program covers the collection, laboratory analysis, and reporting of sediment from the property frontage and the adjacent intertidal flat. It is organized in four parts: preparation of a sampling and analysis plan; a field mobilization to collect surface grabs and shallow cores at the stations that plan fixes; laboratory analysis of the recovered material under chain of custody; and a data report presenting the results, the quality control outcomes, and a comparison against the screening criteria applicable to the setting.

The sampling and analysis plan will be submitted to {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} for review before any field work is scheduled. It will fix station locations and coordinates, sampling depths, the analytical suite, container and preservation requirements, holding times, and the frequency of field duplicates and blanks. Once you accept the plan, it governs the field program. Where the substrate or access forces a change in the field, the change will be recorded in the notebook with the reason for it and carried through into the report. We do not correct a station location quietly and present the result as though nothing happened.

Two things are outside this scope, and I would rather name them than let them be assumed. We are not retained to design a remedy, and we are not retained to speak to an agency on your behalf. If the data support either step, we will tell you so and quote it separately.
Approach

Stations will be located by GPS and occupied on foot during workable low-tide exposure, with the boat used where the flat will not carry a crew. Grabs will be taken from the surface interval named in the plan. Cores will be advanced by hand, logged for recovery, and described for texture, color, odor, and any visible sheen or debris before the target interval is cut out and homogenized. Samples will be placed on ice at the point of collection and delivered under chain of custody within the holding times the analytical suite requires.

Laboratory data will be reviewed against the plan's quality control limits before any result is repeated to you. Where a result is qualified, the qualifier travels with it into every table it appears in. That is not a formality. A number that has lost its qualifier is a number nobody can defend.

You should expect a hedge where the data earn one. Sediment chemistry varies over short distances, and a station tells you about a station. The report will say what was measured, where, and on what date, and it will keep what the results show separate from what they suggest. If you need certainty the sampling density does not support, the answer is more stations, not firmer adjectives.
Project Team

John Sanchez, our Senior Scientist, will lead the technical work and will be your day-to-day contact. He writes the sampling and analysis plan and the data report, and he will be in the field for both events. Heather Morton, Field Technician, will run the field program with him, keep the notebook, and transcribe the station data against it. I will serve as Managing Principal on the engagement. I read every deliverable before it leaves this office and I check the totals in the data tables myself. If you ever want a question answered above John's level, call me directly.
Fee and Commencement

The fee for the scope described above is {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}. That figure covers our labor, field equipment, containers and shipping, the laboratory charges for the suite named in the plan, and the report. It does not cover analyses added after the plan is accepted, a second mobilization made necessary by conditions outside our control, or agency fees. None of those would be incurred without your written authorization first. Work will begin on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}, subject to our receipt of the countersigned copy of this letter and to site access being available.
Fees and Payment

We invoice monthly for work performed and costs incurred, and invoices are payable thirty days from their date. Each invoice shows labor by person and hours, and direct costs at cost. If you question a charge, tell us and we will show you where it came from. Amounts unpaid after sixty days may, on written notice, suspend our work; we would rather call you than send that notice.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from commencement through delivery of the data report. Either of us may terminate it on written notice. If you terminate, you owe us for work performed and for commitments we have already made on your behalf, including laboratory analyses in progress. A sample submitted cannot be unsubmitted. If we terminate, we will deliver the field records and any validated data in hand so that another consultant can pick the work up.
Confidentiality

We hold the data and the report confidential and will not release either to a third party without your written direction, except where disclosure is required by law or by an agency with jurisdiction over the site. In that case we will notify you before we release anything, and as early as the requirement allows. We may describe the general nature of the work in a statement of qualifications without naming Salazar-Mendoza or the site.
Limitation of Liability

Our services will be performed with the care and skill ordinarily used by environmental consultants practicing in this region under similar conditions. No other warranty, express or implied, is made. Conditions between and below sampling stations are inferred from the stations, not observed, and the report will say so. To the fullest extent permitted by law, our aggregate liability to Salazar-Mendoza arising out of this engagement is limited to the fee actually paid to us under it, and neither party is liable to the other for consequential or indirect damages.
Governing Law

This engagement is governed by the law of the state in which the site is located, without regard to its conflict of laws rules. Before either of us files anything, we will sit down with a mutually acceptable mediator and try to settle the matter. In my experience that conversation is usually about a table nobody read carefully, and it is cheaper than the alternative.

If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign below and return one copy to my attention. Questions about scope or schedule are best put to John Sanchez; questions about these terms should come to me. We appreciate the opportunity and will be ready to mobilize on receipt of the signed letter.
Sincerely,
Memorandum

items: To: Heather Morton, Field Technician
Cc: Angela Flynn, Managing Principal
From: John Sanchez, Senior Scientist
Subject: Kickoff, sediment sampling program, Salazar-Mendoza

blocks.text: The letter came back countersigned, so the sediment sampling program for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} is authorized and opens on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}. Nothing has been collected and no station has been fixed. This memo sets the objectives down while they are still cheap to change, and says who is doing what before the tide table starts making the decisions for us.

The objective is narrow and is worth keeping narrow. Surface and shallow subsurface sediment at the frontage and the adjacent flat are to be characterized for chemistry and physical character, well enough that the results survive review by somebody who was not there and is reading them in a file three years from now. That means station locations we can defend, handling we can document, and quality control that is visible in the report rather than asserted in a sentence. We have not been asked what to do about the results. If that question is put to us in the field, it gets written in the notebook and answered from the office.
Workstreams

items: Sampling and analysis plan. Drafted here, read by Angela Flynn, then submitted to Salazar-Mendoza for acceptance before anything is scheduled. It fixes stations and coordinates, depths, the analytical suite, containers and preservation, holding times, quality control frequency, and the screening criteria the data will be compared against. Nothing goes in the field until it comes back accepted.

Access and coordination. Sandra Little is the point of contact for site access, gate and parking arrangements, and any restriction on where a crew may walk. Tide windows are ours to propose; the constraints on them are hers to confirm.

Field program. Two events, each worked around low-tide exposure. Grabs and shallow cores at the plan stations, GPS on every occupation, photographs at every station, and the notebook written at the station rather than in the truck afterward.

Sample handling and laboratory. Chain of custody from the moment a container is filled. Holding times govern the schedule, not the other way around; if a window slips far enough to threaten one, the event moves rather than the paperwork.

Data validation and reporting. Laboratory packages checked against the plan's control limits, qualifiers carried through every table, and a data report that states what was measured, where, and on what date.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Plan draft to Angela Flynn for review before it goes out. She reads it cold, so the station rationale has to be on the page and not in my head.

Heather Morton to pull tide predictions for the candidate windows and mark the ones that give a workable exposure with daylight on both sides of the low. Note the marginal ones as marginal; we would rather see them than have them quietly dropped.
Laboratory capacity and turnaround to be confirmed, and the container order placed once the suite is fixed. No order before the plan is accepted.
Access walk with Sandra Little once the draft stations are on a figure, so that a station nobody can reach is found on paper instead of at low tide.

blocks.text: A word on what this job is likely to turn on. The flat here is not uniform, and the older survey material Salazar-Mendoza sent shows fill in at least one area that the plan will have to account for. Stations sited on fill do not tell you about the sediment the program is meant to characterize, and stations sited to avoid fill can be accused of avoiding the interesting ground. The rationale for each station therefore goes in the plan in writing, before we collect anything, so that it reads as a decision rather than as a result. If the substrate at a station turns out to be other than the plan assumed, the station moves, the move is recorded at the station with its reason, and both locations appear in the report.

Two habits for this job. Write it down when it happens, not that evening over dinner. And if a reading looks wrong, flag it and leave it; do not fix it. An anomaly we can explain is worth a good deal more than a clean sheet we cannot.
Working Session: Sediment Sampling Program
Minutes of a working session held at the site office, {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} program. Taken from the notebook the same afternoon and checked against it, not against memory.
Attendees

items: John Sanchez, Senior Scientist, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC
Heather Morton, Field Technician, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC (minutes)
Sandra Little, Director of Operations, Salazar-Mendoza

blocks.text: Discussion

The first field event was reviewed station by station against the accepted plan. All planned stations on the frontage were occupied. Two stations on the outer flat were shifted shoreward: one because the exposure at the low did not last long enough to work it safely, and one because the substrate at the plan location proved to be riprap fill rather than the soft sediment the plan assumed. Both shifts were recorded at the station with the reason and the new coordinates. John Sanchez noted that the shifted pair are still representative of the interval they were meant to represent, but that the report will show both the planned and the occupied locations rather than only the occupied ones.

Sample condition on delivery was reviewed. Temperatures on receipt were within the plan's acceptance range for every cooler. Chain of custody was intact and no seal was broken. One container arrived with a cracked lid; it was rejected on receipt and the station will be reoccupied during the second event rather than the result being reported from a compromised container.

The first laboratory data packages have come back for part of the suite. The field duplicate at one frontage station returned a relative percent difference above the plan's control limit for one analyte class. Both results are being carried, qualified, pending the remaining packages. John Sanchez was asked whether the exceedance indicated a handling problem and declined to say so on the evidence in hand: heterogeneous sediment produces this without any error in the field, and a single duplicate does not distinguish the two. If the pattern repeats in the second event's duplicates, the homogenization step will be re-examined. It was noted for the record that nothing else in the packages so far is outside the control limits.

Sandra Little asked whether the results seen to date support any statement about the frontage. The answer was that they do not yet. The suite is incomplete, the second event has not been worked, and a partial data set read early tends to get quoted later without its qualifications attached. She accepted this and asked only that anything that would change the shape of the program be raised as it appears rather than saved for the report.

Access for the second event was discussed. The gate arrangement that worked for the first event will be repeated. Sandra Little will confirm whether the contractor working the north end will be on site during the candidate windows, since that access road is the only practical way to reach the two shifted stations with equipment.
Action Items

rows: Circulate the station log for the first event, showing planned and occupied coordinates side by side
Reoccupy the station whose container was rejected on receipt
Track the remaining laboratory packages and flag any further control limit exceedance on arrival
Confirm contractor presence and north access road availability for the candidate windows
Propose second event tide windows once access is confirmed
Re-examine the homogenization step if the second event duplicates repeat the exceedance

blocks.text: No decisions were taken beyond those recorded above. Corrections to these minutes should come to Heather Morton and will be issued as a marked revision rather than a silent replacement.
Status Report: Sediment Sampling Program

items: Prepared for: Salazar-Mendoza
Prepared by: John Sanchez, Senior Scientist
Field staff: Heather Morton, Field Technician

blocks.text: Status Summary

The sediment sampling program for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} is on schedule. The sampling and analysis plan is accepted and has governed the work since. The first field event has been completed and its samples are through the laboratory in part. The second field event is planned and awaiting a confirmed access window. No finding is offered in this report. The suite is incomplete and it would be premature, and unhelpful to you later, to characterize the frontage from a partial data set.
Accomplishments This Period

All planned frontage stations were occupied during the first event and sampled at the intervals the plan specifies. Two outer flat stations were shifted shoreward, one for exposure duration and one because the plan location proved to be riprap fill rather than soft sediment. Planned and occupied coordinates were both recorded and both will appear in the report. The remaining planned stations were occupied as drawn.

Cores were logged for recovery and described for texture, color, odor, and visible debris before the target intervals were cut and homogenized. Photographs were taken at every occupation. The station log was transcribed from the field notebook and checked against it by Heather Morton, who identified two coordinate entries that did not reconcile; both were resolved against the notebook and the GPS track, and the correction is noted in the log rather than written over.

Samples were delivered under chain of custody within holding times. Cooler temperatures on receipt were within the acceptance range in every case. One container was rejected at the laboratory for a cracked lid; that station will be reoccupied during the second event rather than reported from a compromised sample.

A working session was held with your Director of Operations, at which the first event was reviewed station by station against the accepted plan and the sample condition on receipt was walked through. The station log, the departures from plan, and the access arrangements for the second event were covered there and are not repeated at length here.

Part of the analytical suite has returned. The packages received have been checked against the plan's control limits. One field duplicate exceeded the relative percent difference limit for a single analyte class; both results are being carried with qualifiers pending the balance of the data. Nothing else received to date falls outside the control limits.
Upcoming Work

The second field event will be worked once the access window is confirmed and the tide predictions give workable exposure with daylight on both sides of the low. It will cover the reoccupation described above, the balance of the planned stations, and the duplicate and blank frequency the plan requires. The remaining laboratory packages from the first event are expected during the coming period and will be validated as they arrive rather than in a batch at the end, so that a systematic problem, if there is one, is found while a second event can still respond to it. Report drafting will begin once the first event's data set is complete and validated.
Budget

Costs incurred to date are consistent with the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}} and no change to that figure is anticipated for the scope as accepted. The station reoccupation is absorbed within the second event and carries no additional charge. Should the outstanding data indicate that additional analyses are warranted, they would be scoped and authorized in writing before any cost was incurred, as the engagement letter provides.
Risks
Three risks are worth stating plainly, none of them presently material to schedule or fee.

items: Access timing. The second event depends on a window that is both workable at the low and clear of contractor activity on the north access road. The tide constrains the candidates tightly and a lost window pushes the event to the next set rather than to the next day.

Weather during the event. A period of freshwater runoff before sampling can change surface conditions on the flat. It would not invalidate the data, but it would be described in the report and would qualify comparisons across the two events.

Duplicate variability. If the first event's exceedance repeats in the second event's duplicates, the homogenization step will be re-examined. The more likely explanation remains sediment heterogeneity at the station scale, which is a property of the material and not a defect in the work, but the alternative cannot be ruled out from one duplicate and will not be claimed to be.

blocks.text: Nothing observed to date requires a decision from Salazar-Mendoza between now and the second event. Questions on any of the above are welcome before the report is drafted rather than after.
Working Session: Sediment Sampling Program, Draft Report Review
Minutes of a working session on the draft data report, {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} program. Taken from the notebook and checked against it.
Attendees

items: John Sanchez, Senior Scientist, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC
Heather Morton, Field Technician, Saltmarsh Environmental Partners LLC (minutes)
Sandra Little, Director of Operations, Salazar-Mendoza

blocks.text: Discussion

Both field events are complete and the analytical data set is complete and validated. The draft data report was circulated in advance of this session and was walked through section by section. The purpose of the session was to take comments on the draft, not to reopen the scope.

The station tables were reviewed first. Planned and occupied coordinates appear side by side for the two shifted outer flat stations and for the station reoccupied after the container rejection, with the reason for each departure carried in the table rather than in a footnote. Sandra Little asked whether the departures needed that much visibility in a document a reviewer would read years from now. John Sanchez said they did: a reviewer who finds a discrepancy between the plan and the data on his own will distrust everything else in the report, and a departure explained on the page costs a line and buys the rest of the document.

The duplicate exceedance from the first event was discussed again in light of the complete data. The second event duplicates were within the plan's control limits for every analyte class, including the class that exceeded in the first event. The homogenization step was re-examined against the field notes and no deviation from the plan was found. The draft attributes the single exceedance to sediment heterogeneity at the station scale, qualifies both results, and states that a handling cause cannot be excluded on one duplicate. Sandra Little asked whether the hedge could be dropped now that the second event was clean. It could not, and John Sanchez said so: the second event does not tell us what happened at a different station in a different month, and the qualifier stays with the result wherever it appears.

Comparison against screening criteria was reviewed as drafted. The comparison is presented per station and per analyte, without aggregation across stations, and the draft states in the text that conditions between and below stations are inferred rather than observed. Sandra Little noted that her board will want a summary sentence. The draft's summary language was read aloud and she accepted it as written.

Comments on the draft were otherwise editorial: figure legibility at the frontage scale, one mislabeled cross-reference, and consistent use of the station identifiers between the tables and the figures. Sandra Little will send written comments so that they can be logged and dispositioned individually rather than absorbed in conversation. The revised draft goes to Angela Flynn before it goes back out; she reads every deliverable and checks the table totals herself, and comments returned late will wait for that pass rather than skip it.
Action Items

rows: Send written comments on the draft data report
Log and disposition each comment with a written response
Redraw the frontage figures for legibility at the printed scale
Reconcile station identifiers across tables and figures and fix the mislabeled cross-reference
Revised draft to Angela Flynn for review before it goes back to the client
Assemble the field records and validated data packages for the project file

blocks.text: No change to scope or fee arises from this session. Corrections to these minutes should come to Heather Morton and will be issued as a marked revision.
